title: What is Super-Resolution?
concept: Super-Resolution is the task of reconstructing a high-resolution image from a low-resolution input, recovering plausible fine detail that was lost when the image was downscaled or captured at low quality.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is one legitimate engineering use of style transfer besides art filters?

options.text: Sim-to-real domain adaptation for training data

exercises.explain: Yes! Domain adaptation is a real engineering use case.

cards.title: Zooming into a blurry photo
cards.body: You've seen it in movies: a detective 'enhances' a blurry security camera photo until a license plate becomes readable. In reality, no amount of zooming reveals detail that was never captured — but a trained CNN can plausibly reconstruct it.

cards.title: Super-Resolution
cards.body: Super-Resolution (SR) is the task of generating a higher-resolution image from a lower-resolution input, using a model trained to predict plausible fine detail (edges, textures) that matches how real high-res images look.

cards.title: Filling in a puzzle from a blurry photo of it
cards.body: Imagine reconstructing a jigsaw puzzle's fine details from a fuzzy, low-res photo of the completed picture. You can't recover the EXACT original pixels, but a model trained on thousands of similar puzzles can make an educated, realistic guess at the missing detail.

cards.title: Smartphone camera zoom
cards.body: Modern phone cameras use super-resolution to make digital zoom look sharper: the sensor captures a low-res crop, and an on-device SR model reconstructs plausible extra detail before displaying the zoomed photo.

cards.title: It's a plausible guess, not magic recovery
cards.body: SR models don't literally recover information that was destroyed — they generate a statistically plausible high-res image based on patterns learned from millions of real photos. Two different SR models can 'enhance' the same blurry input into two different (both plausible) outputs.

left.text:
Fewer pixels (e.g. 64x64)
Blurry, blocky edges
Missing fine texture

options.text: Reconstructs a plausible higher-resolution image from a lower-resolution input
Compresses an image to a smaller file size
Detects objects and draws bounding boxes
Converts a color image to grayscale

exercises.explain: Correct! SR generates a plausible high-resolution reconstruction.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Super-Resolution literally recovers the exact original pixels that were lost.
exercises.explain: False. It generates a statistically plausible reconstruction, not the literal original data.

exercises.sentence: Super-Resolution reconstructs plausible fine ___ that was lost in a low-resolution image.
exercises.explain: SR models predict plausible edges and textures based on learned patterns.

exercises.prompt: Why can two different SR models produce two different 'enhanced' outputs from the same blurry input?

options.text: Because each model makes its own plausible statistical guess based on what it learned from training data
Because SR models always produce identical outputs by design
Because blurry images have no valid enhanced output

exercises.explain: Correct! Each model's guess reflects its own training data and architecture.

exercises.prompt: A journalist wants to use SR to 'read' a license plate from an extremely blurry surveillance photo for a legal case. What is the key risk?

options.text: The SR output is a plausible guess, not guaranteed truth — using it as factual evidence could be misleading or wrong
SR guarantees 100% pixel-perfect accuracy for any input
SR only works on color images, never grayscale surveillance footage
There is no risk; enhanced images are always legally admissible

exercises.explain: Exactly! SR reconstructs plausible detail, not verified fact — a critical distinction for high-stakes use. Next: the first deep learning approach to this problem, SRCNN.
